texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nubra is a beautiful region in Ladakh. An eleven-year old girl Yangdol and her twin brother Dorjay live in one of the villages of this region.
texts.text: Nubra is a beautiful region in Ladakh. An eleven-year old girl Yangdol and her twin brother Dorjay live in one of the villages of this region.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Nubra in Ladakh, India
texts.text: Nubra in Ladakh, India

texts.label: text
texts.orig: They love their surroundings—the majestic mountain peaks, and the glaciers, but their favourite is the night sky when the entire sky is lit up with thousands of stars (Fig. 12.1). The weather in Nubra is almost cloudless. With almost no air or light pollution, the night sky is very clearly visible. Night after night, Yangdol and Dorjay observe the stars and experience an immense sense of awe.
texts.text: They love their surroundings—the majestic mountain peaks, and the glaciers, but their favourite is the night sky when the entire sky is lit up with thousands of stars (Fig. 12.1). The weather in Nubra is almost cloudless. With almost no air or light pollution, the night sky is very clearly visible. Night after night, Yangdol and Dorjay observe the stars and experience an immense sense of awe.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 12.1: The beauty of night sky from a very dark location in Ladakh, India
texts.text: Fig. 12.1: The beauty of night sky from a very dark location in Ladakh, India

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Growing up, Yangdol and Dorjay have been hearing interesting stories about stars from their elders. They have heard how some particular stars in the clear skies helped the caravans passing through Nubra in finding direction in the ancient days. They wonder how far away and how big the stars are. They also enjoy trying to find some patterns among the stars that remind them of familiar objects. Have you ever looked at the stars in the night sky and tried to connect them with imaginary lines, just like dots and lines in a drawing?
texts.text: Growing up, Yangdol and Dorjay have been hearing interesting stories about stars from their elders. They have heard how some particular stars in the clear skies helped the caravans passing through Nubra in finding direction in the ancient days. They wonder how far away and how big the stars are. They also enjoy trying to find some patterns among the stars that remind them of familiar objects. Have you ever looked at the stars in the night sky and tried to connect them with imaginary lines, just like dots and lines in a drawing?

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 12.1 Stars and Constellations
texts.text: 12.1 Stars and Constellations

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At night, when we look up at the sky, we see many stars. Some stars are bright and others are dim. Stars shine with their own light.
texts.text: At night, when we look up at the sky, we see many stars. Some stars are bright and others are dim. Stars shine with their own light.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some groups of stars appear to form patterns which are like shapes of familiar things. Long ago, when watching stars in the night sky was a favourite pastime of our ancestors, they identified these star patterns with animals, things or characters in stories. Many cultures had names for patterns based on their own stories. These imaginary shapes helped them in recognising stars in the sky. some
texts.text: Some groups of stars appear to form patterns which are like shapes of familiar things. Long ago, when watching stars in the night sky was a favourite pastime of our ancestors, they identified these star patterns with animals, things or characters in stories. Many cultures had names for patterns based on their own stories. These imaginary shapes helped them in recognising stars in the sky. some

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Do we find patterns among the stars just for fun or is there use of these patterns?
texts.text: Do we find patterns among the stars just for fun or is there use of these patterns?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recognising stars and their patterns was a useful skill for navigation in the olden times. Before the arrival of modern technology or even before the invention of the magnetic compass, it helped people, particularly sailors and travellers, in finding directions at sea or on land. It is still used in emergencies as a backup method.
texts.text: Recognising stars and their patterns was a useful skill for navigation in the olden times. Before the arrival of modern technology or even before the invention of the magnetic compass, it helped people, particularly sailors and travellers, in finding directions at sea or on land. It is still used in emergencies as a backup method.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In earlier times, groups of stars forming patterns were called constellations. Currently, the regions of sky, which include these groups of stars, are defined as constellations. However, since in constellations, the patterns of stars are often the most prominent, the term constellation is still commonly used for these groups of stars.
texts.text: In earlier times, groups of stars forming patterns were called constellations. Currently, the regions of sky, which include these groups of stars, are defined as constellations. However, since in constellations, the patterns of stars are often the most prominent, the term constellation is still commonly used for these groups of stars.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Some constellations are shown in Fig. 12.3. The stars are joined by imaginary lines, drawn for easy identification. The constellation Orion is often represented as a hunter. There are three stars in the middle, which represent the belt of the hunter. Some people imagine that the hunter Orion, followed by his dog (constellation Canis Major), is battling a bull (constellation Taurus). Canis Major contains a star
texts.text: Some constellations are shown in Fig. 12.3. The stars are joined by imaginary lines, drawn for easy identification. The constellation Orion is often represented as a hunter. There are three stars in the middle, which represent the belt of the hunter. Some people imagine that the hunter Orion, followed by his dog (constellation Canis Major), is battling a bull (constellation Taurus). Canis Major contains a star

texts.label: text
texts.orig: called Sirius, which is the brightest star in the night sky (Fig. 12.3).
texts.text: called Sirius, which is the brightest star in the night sky (Fig. 12.3).

texts.orig: Fig. 12.3: Some constellations and stars
texts.text: Fig. 12.3: Some constellations and stars

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (The red lines indicate the patterns of stars while the green lines indicate the regions of the sky. The lines are not seen in the sky and have been drawn only for easy identification)
texts.text: (The red lines indicate the patterns of stars while the green lines indicate the regions of the sky. The lines are not seen in the sky and have been drawn only for easy identification)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Indian astronomy, the term nakshatra is used to denote either a certain star or a group of stars, such as Ardra (a star called Betelgeuse in the Orion constellation) and krittika (a group of stars called Pleiades in the Taurus constellation). Aldebaran, a star in the constellation Taurus, is known as Rohini.
texts.text: In Indian astronomy, the term nakshatra is used to denote either a certain star or a group of stars, such as Ardra (a star called Betelgeuse in the Orion constellation) and krittika (a group of stars called Pleiades in the Taurus constellation). Aldebaran, a star in the constellation Taurus, is known as Rohini.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two distinct patterns of stars, the Big Dipper and the Little Dipper, are shown in Fig. 12.4. The Pole Star or Polaris, which is part of the Little Dipper is also shown (Fig. 12.4).
texts.text: Two distinct patterns of stars, the Big Dipper and the Little Dipper, are shown in Fig. 12.4. The Pole Star or Polaris, which is part of the Little Dipper is also shown (Fig. 12.4).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Pole Star appears stationary in the North direction, which helps to locate the North direction in the Northern hemisphere.
texts.text: The Pole Star appears stationary in the North direction, which helps to locate the North direction in the Northern hemisphere.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 12.4: Big Dipper, Little Dipper and Pole Star (The lines are not seen in the sky and have been drawn only for easy identification) yf » i
texts.text: Fig. 12.4: Big Dipper, Little Dipper and Pole Star (The lines are not seen in the sky and have been drawn only for easy identification) yf » i

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Big Dipper lies in the constellation Ursa Major while the Little Dipper lies in the constellation Ursa Minor. In India, the Big Dipper is known as Saptarishi, and the Pole Star is known as Dhruva tara.
texts.text: The Big Dipper lies in the constellation Ursa Major while the Little Dipper lies in the constellation Ursa Minor. In India, the Big Dipper is known as Saptarishi, and the Pole Star is known as Dhruva tara.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: | 2 2 Night Sky Watching
texts.text: | 2 2 Night Sky Watching

texts.label: text
texts.orig: If it is a clear cloudless night, a large number of stars may be visible in the sky. If you stay in a big city, you may find that the sky is rarely clear and only a few stars are seen in the night sky. This is due to light pollution, smoke and dust. The presence of excessive artificial light at night time is referred to as light pollution. In villages or areas where there is less light pollution, a larger number of stars can be seen. Also, your house may be surrounded by tall buildings and trees, which may block your view. The night sky is best viewed from open dark areas.
texts.text: If it is a clear cloudless night, a large number of stars may be visible in the sky. If you stay in a big city, you may find that the sky is rarely clear and only a few stars are seen in the night sky. This is due to light pollution, smoke and dust. The presence of excessive artificial light at night time is referred to as light pollution. In villages or areas where there is less light pollution, a larger number of stars can be seen. Also, your house may be surrounded by tall buildings and trees, which may block your view. The night sky is best viewed from open dark areas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Can we try to find any constellation or star of our choice on any night from our location?
texts.text: Can we try to find any constellation or star of our choice on any night from our location?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Not all stars and constellations are visible from all places on Earth and on all nights in a year. For example, the Pole Star is not visible from the southern hemisphere of Earth. To identify a star or a constellation, you need to know how a particular constellation looks like and where to look for it in the night sky. To get familiar with the pattern of a constellation you may use images such as Fig. 12.3 and Fig. 12.4. . To find out when and in which portion of the sky a star or ~ a constellation will be visible from your location, you may take the help of sky mapping apps that can be downloaded on a mobile phone, or other online resources.
texts.text: Not all stars and constellations are visible from all places on Earth and on all nights in a year. For example, the Pole Star is not visible from the southern hemisphere of Earth. To identify a star or a constellation, you need to know how a particular constellation looks like and where to look for it in the night sky. To get familiar with the pattern of a constellation you may use images such as Fig. 12.3 and Fig. 12.4. . To find out when and in which portion of the sky a star or ~ a constellation will be visible from your location, you may take the help of sky mapping apps that can be downloaded on a mobile phone, or other online resources.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Preparation for night sky watching
texts.text: Preparation for night sky watching

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: a4 Under the guidance of adults, identify a dark open area for night sky watching. This should be away from lights, tall buildings and trees.
texts.text: a4 Under the guidance of adults, identify a dark open area for night sky watching. This should be away from lights, tall buildings and trees.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Choose the date and time based upon what you plan to identify in the night sky.
texts.text: Choose the date and time based upon what you plan to identify in the night sky.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: Choose a moonless night with no clouds, particularly for the Pole Star which is not very bright.
texts.text: Choose a moonless night with no clouds, particularly for the Pole Star which is not very bright.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: It may be useful to have access to a mobile app witha sky map, or print out images of the constellations you plan to view. You may also carry a magnetic compass to find directions and a notebook to note or draw your observations.
texts.text: It may be useful to have access to a mobile app witha sky map, or print out images of the constellations you plan to view. You may also carry a magnetic compass to find directions and a notebook to note or draw your observations.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: On the particular day and time selected, go to the identified place with an adult, where the night sky watching is to be done.
texts.text: On the particular day and time selected, go to the identified place with an adult, where the night sky watching is to be done.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: After reaching there, wait for about half an hour for your eyes to get adjusted to the darkness. This will help you to see the night sky better (Fig. 12.5).
texts.text: After reaching there, wait for about half an hour for your eyes to get adjusted to the darkness. This will help you to see the night sky better (Fig. 12.5).

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 12.5: Night sky watching
texts.text: Fig. 12.5: Night sky watching

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You may easily identify the Big Dipper and the Pole Star in the night sky.
texts.text: You may easily identify the Big Dipper and the Pole Star in the night sky.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 12.2: Let us try to locate
texts.text: Activity 12.2: Let us try to locate

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Look for the Big Dipper during summer time in the early part of the night, say, around 9 p.m. View the sky above the horizon towards the northern part of the sky and identify the Big Dipper.
texts.text: ¢ Look for the Big Dipper during summer time in the early part of the night, say, around 9 p.m. View the sky above the horizon towards the northern part of the sky and identify the Big Dipper.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Once you identify the Big Dipper, try to locate the Pole Star. Look at the two stars present at the end of the Big Dipper's cup and imagine a straight line passing through these towards the north. At about five times the distance between these two stars, the imaginary line will lead to another star which is not very bright. This star is the Pole Star.
texts.text: ¢ Once you identify the Big Dipper, try to locate the Pole Star. Look at the two stars present at the end of the Big Dipper's cup and imagine a straight line passing through these towards the north. At about five times the distance between these two stars, the imaginary line will lead to another star which is not very bright. This star is the Pole Star.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You may also locate the bright constellation Orion and the star Sirius in the night sky.
texts.text: You may also locate the bright constellation Orion and the star Sirius in the night sky.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Activity 12.3: Let us try to identify
texts.text: Activity 12.3: Let us try to identify

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ In India, Orion is best viewed during the months of December to April after sunset. So, look for it during that period.
texts.text: ¢ In India, Orion is best viewed during the months of December to April after sunset. So, look for it during that period.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ Three bright stars in a short straight line are located around the middle of Orion (imagined to be the belt of a hunter). Identify these three stars first, as this is the easiest way to find Orion.
texts.text: ¢ Three bright stars in a short straight line are located around the middle of Orion (imagined to be the belt of a hunter). Identify these three stars first, as this is the easiest way to find Orion.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ Once you identify Orion, it is easy to locate the very bright star Sirius which is located close to Orion. Imagine a straight line passing through the three middle stars of Orion and look along this line towards the east. This will lead to Sirius.
texts.text: @ Once you identify Orion, it is easy to locate the very bright star Sirius which is located close to Orion. Imagine a straight line passing through the three middle stars of Orion and look along this line towards the east. This will lead to Sirius.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 12.3 Our Solar System
texts.text: 12.3 Our Solar System

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: The Sun
texts.text: The Sun

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Sun is a star. It is the star closest to us. It is an extremely hot spherical ball of gases. The Sun gives out a huge amount of energy, and that is why it glows so brightly.
texts.text: The Sun is a star. It is the star closest to us. It is an extremely hot spherical ball of gases. The Sun gives out a huge amount of energy, and that is why it glows so brightly.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Sun produces heat and light, and is the main source of energy on the Earth (Fig. 12.6).
texts.text: The Sun produces heat and light, and is the main source of energy on the Earth (Fig. 12.6).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: How big is the Sun? It is about 100 times bigger than the Earth in diameter. And still, it looks so small because it is very far from the Earth.
texts.text: How big is the Sun? It is about 100 times bigger than the Earth in diameter. And still, it looks so small because it is very far from the Earth.

texts.orig: Fig. 12.6: The rising Sun

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are many more objects in the sky. Our Earth, along with some of these objects, and the Sun together form our Solar System (Fig. 12.7). Most of th biect ost of these objects move around the Sun. The movement of an object around the Sun is called revolution.
texts.text: There are many more objects in the sky. Our Earth, along with some of these objects, and the Sun together form our Solar System (Fig. 12.7). Most of th biect ost of these objects move around the Sun. The movement of an object around the Sun is called revolution.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Planets
texts.text: Planets

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A planet is a large, nearly spherical object that revolves around the Sun. Our Earth is a planet as it revolves around the Sun as shown in Fig. 12.7. The Earth takes nearly one year to complete one revolution. Like the Earth, there are other planets that revolve around the Sun.
texts.text: A planet is a large, nearly spherical object that revolves around the Sun. Our Earth is a planet as it revolves around the Sun as shown in Fig. 12.7. The Earth takes nearly one year to complete one revolution. Like the Earth, there are other planets that revolve around the Sun.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Are stars the only objects in the sky? Or, are there more objects which we may not have noticed?
texts.text: Are stars the only objects in the sky? Or, are there more objects which we may not have noticed?

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 12.7: An artist's representation of the Solar System
texts.text: Fig. 12.7: An artist's representation of the Solar System

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (To fit this figure within the page, the sizes and distances of various objects are not as per their actual sizes and distances in relation to each other, that is, the sizes and distances are not to scale in the figure)
texts.text: (To fit this figure within the page, the sizes and distances of various objects are not as per their actual sizes and distances in relation to each other, that is, the sizes and distances are not to scale in the figure)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The eight planets, in order of their increasing distance from the Sun, are Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune (Fig. 12.7).
texts.text: The eight planets, in order of their increasing distance from the Sun, are Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune (Fig. 12.7).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The inner four planets nearest to the Sun—Mercury, Venus, Earth and Mars—are smaller in size. They have solid surfaces with rocks on them.
texts.text: The inner four planets nearest to the Sun—Mercury, Venus, Earth and Mars—are smaller in size. They have solid surfaces with rocks on them.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From ancient times, various names have been used across India for the planets visible to the naked eye. For example, Budha (Mercury), Shukra (Venus), Prithvi (Earth), Mangala (Mars), Brihaspati or Guru (Jupiter) and Shani (Saturn).
texts.text: From ancient times, various names have been used across India for the planets visible to the naked eye. For example, Budha (Mercury), Shukra (Venus), Prithvi (Earth), Mangala (Mars), Brihaspati or Guru (Jupiter) and Shani (Saturn).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Venus is usually seen shining brightly at dawn and dusk, and is commonly called the Morning Star or the Evening Star, even though it is not a star. Mars is called the Red Planet because it appears red. This is because the soil on Mars is reddish in colour.
texts.text: Venus is usually seen shining brightly at dawn and dusk, and is commonly called the Morning Star or the Evening Star, even though it is not a star. Mars is called the Red Planet because it appears red. This is because the soil on Mars is reddish in colour.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A large portion of the Earth's surface is covered with water and thus, it appears blue from the space. Due to this, the Earth is also called the Blue Planet.
texts.text: A large portion of the Earth's surface is covered with water and thus, it appears blue from the space. Due to this, the Earth is also called the Blue Planet.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The four outermost planets—Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus and Neptune—are much larger compared to the Earth, and are mostly made of gases. These giant gaseous planets have large flat ring-like structures around them which are made of dust particles and rocky material.
texts.text: The four outermost planets—Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus and Neptune—are much larger compared to the Earth, and are mostly made of gases. These giant gaseous planets have large flat ring-like structures around them which are made of dust particles and rocky material.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Planets get most of their energy from the Sun. So, the farther they are, the colder they are in general. The presence of an atmosphere on a planet can trap heat which can significantly change the temperature of a planet. That is why Venus, for example, is hotter than Mercury, although it is farther from the Sun.
texts.text: Planets get most of their energy from the Sun. So, the farther they are, the colder they are in general. The presence of an atmosphere on a planet can trap heat which can significantly change the temperature of a planet. That is why Venus, for example, is hotter than Mercury, although it is farther from the Sun.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Among the planets, it is easiest to identify Venus which is very bright. After the Sun and the Moon, Venus is the brightest object in the sky. Mercury, Mars, Jupiter and Saturn can also be seen with the naked eye. They are
texts.text: Among the planets, it is easiest to identify Venus which is very bright. After the Sun and the Moon, Venus is the brightest object in the sky. Mercury, Mars, Jupiter and Saturn can also be seen with the naked eye. They are

texts.label: text
texts.orig: so far away that they look like shining dots, just like stars. So how can we distinguish between planets and the stars? Unlike planets, the stars appear to twinkle a lot.
texts.text: so far away that they look like shining dots, just like stars. So how can we distinguish between planets and the stars? Unlike planets, the stars appear to twinkle a lot.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: ¢ For most of the year, Venus can be located either at dawn or at dusk.
texts.text: ¢ For most of the year, Venus can be located either at dawn or at dusk.

texts.orig: @ When you are viewing at dawn, look for it near the Eastern direction before sunrise.
texts.text: @ When you are viewing at dawn, look for it near the Eastern direction before sunrise.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: How can we see the planets which are not visible to the naked eye?
texts.text: How can we see the planets which are not visible to the naked eye?

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: @ When you are viewing at dusk, look for it near the Western direction after sunset.
texts.text: @ When you are viewing at dusk, look for it near the Western direction after sunset.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While many of the objects in the sky can be seen with our naked eyes directly, we can see them brighter and larger using a pair of binoculars or an instrument called a telescope (Fig. 12.8). A telescope also helps us view many dim objects not visible by our naked eyes directly.
texts.text: While many of the objects in the sky can be seen with our naked eyes directly, we can see them brighter and larger using a pair of binoculars or an instrument called a telescope (Fig. 12.8). A telescope also helps us view many dim objects not visible by our naked eyes directly.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: You may get an opportunity to view the sky through a telescope whenever a night sky watching event is organised in your region.
texts.text: You may get an opportunity to view the sky through a telescope whenever a night sky watching event is organised in your region.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We know that planets move around the Sun. Are there any objects that move around planets?
texts.text: We know that planets move around the Sun. Are there any objects that move around planets?

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 12.8: A telescope
texts.text: Fig. 12.8: A telescope

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Natural Satellites
texts.text: Natural Satellites

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Objects that move around planets are commonly called satellites. They are smaller in size than planets. Moons are natural satellites of planets. The Earth has one Moon, while Mars has two moons. Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus and Neptune have a large number of moons.
texts.text: Objects that move around planets are commonly called satellites. They are smaller in size than planets. Moons are natural satellites of planets. The Earth has one Moon, while Mars has two moons. Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus and Neptune have a large number of moons.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 12.9: The Moon (a) Image taken by Cartosat, ISRO. (b) Close-up image by cameras onboard Chandrayaan-3, ISRO.
texts.text: Fig. 12.9: The Moon (a) Image taken by Cartosat, ISRO. (b) Close-up image by cameras onboard Chandrayaan-3, ISRO.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: The Moon
texts.text: The Moon

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Earth's natural satellite, the Moon, takes about 27 days to complete one revolution around the Earth. It is our nearest neighbour in the space. Unlike on the Earth, there is hardly any atmosphere on the Moon. How big is the Moon as compared to the Earth? The Moon is about a quarter the size of the Earth in diameter. The Moon's surface shows circular bowl-like structures called craters (Fig. 12.9). Most of these craters have been formed due to the impact of asteroids or rocks from the space hitting the Moon's surface. Since there is no
texts.text: Earth's natural satellite, the Moon, takes about 27 days to complete one revolution around the Earth. It is our nearest neighbour in the space. Unlike on the Earth, there is hardly any atmosphere on the Moon. How big is the Moon as compared to the Earth? The Moon is about a quarter the size of the Earth in diameter. The Moon's surface shows circular bowl-like structures called craters (Fig. 12.9). Most of these craters have been formed due to the impact of asteroids or rocks from the space hitting the Moon's surface. Since there is no

texts.label: text
texts.orig: atmosphere, water or life on the Moon, these features stay on the Moon's surface for a very long time.
texts.text: atmosphere, water or life on the Moon, these features stay on the Moon's surface for a very long time.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Although the Moon is very far, humans have sent spacecrafts to explore and understand more about the Moon. India has also launched three Chandrayaan missions to study the Moon, and a further mission is being planned.
texts.text: Although the Moon is very far, humans have sent spacecrafts to explore and understand more about the Moon. India has also launched three Chandrayaan missions to study the Moon, and a further mission is being planned.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Asteroids
texts.text: Asteroids

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Comets
texts.text: Comets

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sometimes we have visitors from the outer regions of the Solar System! These objects with long tails are called comets (Fig. 12.10). They are made up of dust, gases, rocks and ice. As a comet approaches close to the Sun, the frozen material in it starts evaporating. This evaporating
texts.text: Sometimes we have visitors from the outer regions of the Solar System! These objects with long tails are called comets (Fig. 12.10). They are made up of dust, gases, rocks and ice. As a comet approaches close to the Sun, the frozen material in it starts evaporating. This evaporating

texts.label: text
texts.orig: material forms the tail of the comet. Comets appear dim as they move away from the Sun and then, it is not possible to see them with the naked eye.
texts.text: material forms the tail of the comet. Comets appear dim as they move away from the Sun and then, it is not possible to see them with the naked eye.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 12.10: A Comet
texts.text: Fig. 12.10: A Comet

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One famous comet is the Halley's Comet which appears every 76 years. Its last appearance was in 1986.
texts.text: One famous comet is the Halley's Comet which appears every 76 years. Its last appearance was in 1986.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In Sanskrit and a few other Indian languages, a comet is called Dhumaketu.
texts.text: In Sanskrit and a few other Indian languages, a comet is called Dhumaketu.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Various tribes in India also call it Pucchya-Taro (star with a tail) or ZendyaTaro (star like a flag).
texts.text: Various tribes in India also call it Pucchya-Taro (star with a tail) or ZendyaTaro (star like a flag).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In many cultures, comets have been feared and it was earlier believed that comets bring bad luck. However, thanks to scientists, we now know that these are just icy-rocky visitors making a trip close to the Sun!
texts.text: In many cultures, comets have been feared and it was earlier believed that comets bring bad luck. However, thanks to scientists, we now know that these are just icy-rocky visitors making a trip close to the Sun!

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Many comets have been found that revolve around the Sun. These comets approach close to the Sun periodically. However, there are a few comets that escape and move out of the Solar System. Few other comets get broken up, or fall into the Sun or other planets when they approach them.
texts.text: Many comets have been found that revolve around the Sun. These comets approach close to the Sun periodically. However, there are a few comets that escape and move out of the Solar System. Few other comets get broken up, or fall into the Sun or other planets when they approach them.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have learnt about the objects which form the Solar System. Which are these objects? The Sun, eight planets, their moons, and many _ smaller objects including asteroids, and comets, together form the Solar System.
texts.text: We have learnt about the objects which form the Solar System. Which are these objects? The Sun, eight planets, their moons, and many _ smaller objects including asteroids, and comets, together form the Solar System.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Sun—our star—is the largest and the heaviest object in the Solar System. The Sun produces almost all the energy in the Solar System. All other objects in our Solar System shine due to the sunlight that they reflect from their surfaces.
texts.text: The Sun—our star—is the largest and the heaviest object in the Solar System. The Sun produces almost all the energy in the Solar System. All other objects in our Solar System shine due to the sunlight that they reflect from their surfaces.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the moonless night sky, viewed from dark locations away from city lights, one can see an extended faint band of light from near north to south across the sky (Fig. 12.11). This is our home galaxy called the Milky Way Galaxy or Akasha Ganga. A galaxy has millions to billions of stars. Our Solar System is part of the Milky Way Galaxy.
texts.text: In the moonless night sky, viewed from dark locations away from city lights, one can see an extended faint band of light from near north to south across the sky (Fig. 12.11). This is our home galaxy called the Milky Way Galaxy or Akasha Ganga. A galaxy has millions to billions of stars. Our Solar System is part of the Milky Way Galaxy.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Fig. 12.11: Milky Way Galaxy as seen from a very dark location in Ladakh, India
texts.text: Fig. 12.11: Milky Way Galaxy as seen from a very dark location in Ladakh, India

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There are many galaxies in the outer space beyond the Milk pace beyond the Milly Way Galaxy. Scientists study them to understand the stars, galaxies and the universe.
texts.text: There are many galaxies in the outer space beyond the Milky Way Galaxy. Scientists study them to understand the stars, galaxies and the universe.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We do not know yet if life exists elsewhere in the Universe. The search for life has been directed mostly towards exoplanets—the planets discovered revolving around other stars in our galaxy. Till now, scientists have not found any evidence of life but this search is on and continuing.
texts.text: We do not know yet if life exists elsewhere in the Universe. The search for life has been directed mostly towards exoplanets—the planets discovered revolving around other stars in our galaxy. Till now, scientists have not found any evidence of life but this search is on and continuing.

texts.text: During a clear night, try to observe the Big Dipper 3-4 times at an interval of 2 to 3 hours. Also try to locate the Pole Star each time. Does the Big Dipper appear to move? Draw a rough sketch to illustrate this, mentioning the time in each case.

texts.orig: Try to find out the names of planets in your local language. Also, find out the stories associated with stars and constellations in your region. Present these stories in a pictorial form.

texts.orig: If there is a planetarium or a science museum nearby, then you can visit it, especially if a night sky observation session is scheduled by them. You will get to see the Moon, the planets and the stars through a telescope. If you visit the planetarium during daytime, you can also see their models, pictures and sky shows.
texts.text: If there is a planetarium or a science museum nearby, then you can visit it, especially if a night sky observation session is scheduled by them. You will get to see the Moon, the planets and the stars through a telescope. If you visit the planetarium during daytime, you can also see their models, pictures and sky shows.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Indian Astronomical Observatory, Hanle, is situated atop the highest peak of Digparatsa-Ri mountain range in Hanle. This peak has been renamed Mount Saraswati.
texts.text: Indian Astronomical Observatory, Hanle, is situated atop the highest peak of Digparatsa-Ri mountain range in Hanle. This peak has been renamed Mount Saraswati.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It has several telescopes. One of them is called the Himalayan Chandra Telescope, which has been named after the Nobel
texts.text: It has several telescopes. One of them is called the Himalayan Chandra Telescope, which has been named after the Nobel

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Prize winning scientist Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar. It is an observatory at one of the highest locations in the world.
texts.text: Prize winning scientist Subrahmanyan Chandrasekhar. It is an observatory at one of the highest locations in the world.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The area surrounding this observatory was notified as the Hanle Dark Sky Reserve (HDSR) in December 2022. The reserve is open to the public throughout the year. Local people have been provided with small telescopes and are trained to be Astronomy
texts.text: The area surrounding this observatory was notified as the Hanle Dark Sky Reserve (HDSR) in December 2022. The reserve is open to the public throughout the year. Local people have been provided with small telescopes and are trained to be Astronomy

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: It is not the end, my friend!
texts.text: It is not the end, my friend!

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This might be the last page of this book, but it is certainly not the end of our 'Curiosity'. The title of this book not only drives our journey through the amazing world of science, but also defines what it means to be human. As a species, we are curious. We want to find out more about the world we live in and worlds beyond. By now, you have perhaps read a little bit about the world of plants and animals, done some fun activities, learnt about how we measure, wondered about the stars that shine in the night sky, and so on. But remember, this is just the beginning. Science is a never-ending adventure, with new discoveries coming up all the time. And as you progress through the Middle Stage, the things you come across will help you find out even more about the world around you. The key lies in that curiosity you have nurtured throughout this journey. Keep observing the world around you, keep asking questions, and do not be afraid to experiment. Remember, even the most groundbreaking discoveries often begin with a simple "Why?". There are countless questions waiting to be answered, and countless answers waiting to be questioned. You, my young scientist, can find them out. So, go forth and explore! And see you again in the next Grade for more adventures in science!
texts.text: This might be the last page of this book, but it is certainly not the end of our 'Curiosity'. The title of this book not only drives our journey through the amazing world of science, but also defines what it means to be human. As a species, we are curious. We want to find out more about the world we live in and worlds beyond. By now, you have perhaps read a little bit about the world of plants and animals, done some fun activities, learnt about how we measure, wondered about the stars that shine in the night sky, and so on. But remember, this is just the beginning. Science is a never-ending adventure, with new discoveries coming up all the time. And as you progress through the Middle Stage, the things you come across will help you find out even more about the world around you. The key lies in that curiosity you have nurtured throughout this journey. Keep observing the world around you, keep asking questions, and do not be afraid to experiment. Remember, even the most groundbreaking discoveries often begin with a simple "Why?". There are countless questions waiting to be answered, and countless answers waiting to be questioned. You, my young scientist, can find them out. So, go forth and explore! And see you again in the next Grade for more adventures in science!

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Which star is closest to
texts.text: Which star is closest to us?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Sun and the planets are nearly spherical in shape. There are many small objects in the Solar System which are rocky and irregular in shape. These are called asteroids. Many of these asteroids revolve around the Sun in paths that lie between those of Mars and Jupiter. This region is called the asteroid belt (Fig. 12.7). Occasionally, asteroids pass very close to the Earth.
texts.text: The Sun and the planets are nearly spherical in shape. There are many small objects in the Solar System which are rocky and irregular in shape. These are called asteroids. Many of these asteroids revolve around the Sun in paths that lie between those of Mars The sizes of and Jupiter. This region is called the asteroids are asteroid belt (Fig. 12.7). Occasionally, from 10 m to asteroids pass very close to the Earth. about 500 kim.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The distance of the Sun from the Earth is about 150 million km. A useful unit for expressing distances within the solar system is 'astronomical unit' (au) which is approximately the distance between the Sun and the Earth.
texts.text: The distance of the Sun from the Earth is about 150 million km. A useful unit for expressing distances within the solar system is 'astronomical unit' (au) which is approximately the distance between the Sun and the Earth.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since different cultures denoted the constellation boundaries in different ways, an internationally agreed set of constellation boundaries were defined by the International Astronomical Union (IAU) in the early twentieth century. 88 constellations were officially listed, thus, dividing the entire sky into 88 regions. These regions of sky are now defined as constellations.
texts.text: Since different cultures denoted the constellation boundaries in different ways, an internationally agreed set of constellation boundaries were defined by the International Astronomical Union (IAU) in the early twentieth century. 88 constellations were officially listed, thus, dividing the entire sky into 88 regions. These regions of sky are now defined as constellations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Light pollution is growing sharply globally. This is reducing our ability to enjoy and study objects in the night sky. Some dark sky reserves and parks have been established J around the world. Light pollution in the reserves is controlled to preserve dark skies for research. There are a few organisations which are working to educate you _ people about reducing light pollution.
texts.text: Light pollution is growing sharply globally. This is reducing our ability to enjoy and study objects in the night sky. Some dark sky reserves and parks have been established J around the world. Light pollution in the reserves is controlled to preserve dark skies for research. There are a few organisations which are working to educate you _ people about reducing light pollution.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Sky Map is a very handy app for identification of stars, constellations and planets from mobile phones. Stellarium is another such app. The computer version of Stellarium is free for download and has many features.
texts.text: Sky Map is a very handy app for identification of stars, constellations and planets from mobile phones. Stellarium is another such app. The computer version of Stellarium is free for download and has many features.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The star nearest to us after the Sun is Proxima Centauri which lies at a distance of about 269000 au. This means that its distance isabout 269000 times our distance to the Sun.
texts.text: The star nearest to us after the Sun is Proxima Centauri which lies at a distance of about 269000 au. This means that its distance isabout 269000 times our distance to the Sun.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While revolving around the Sun, the Earth is also rotating about its axis. For one full rotation, the Earth takes about 24 hours which is called a day. Like the Earth, other planets also rotate about their axes while revolving around the Sun. You ae will learn more about this in the next Grade.
texts.text: While revolving around the Sun, the Earth is also rotating about its axis. For one full rotation, the Earth takes about 24 hours which is called a day. Like the Earth, other planets also rotate about their axes while revolving around the Sun. You ae will learn more about this in the next Grade.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: There is another object called Pluto that lies farther than Neptune, and revolves around the Sun. It is smaller than the Earth's Moon. When it was discovered, it was called a planet of the Solar
texts.text: There is another object called Pluto that lies farther than Neptune, and revolves around the Sun. It is smaller than the Earth's Moon. When it was discovered, it was called a planet of the Solar

texts.label: text
texts.orig: System. But later, when similar more small objects were discovered, the International Astronomical Union (IAU) in 2006 redefined the requirements of an object to be called a planet. As per this definition, these smaller objects, including Pluto, are now called dwarf planets.
texts.text: System. But later, when similar more small objects were discovered, the International Astronomical Union (IAU) in 2006 redefined the requirements of an object to be called a planet. As per this definition, these smaller objects, including Pluto, are now called dwarf planets.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Moon is about 3,84,000 km away from the Earth.
texts.text: The Moon is about 3,84,000 km away from the Earth.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To improve our understanding of the Moon, India's first mission to the Moon, Chandrayaan-1, was launched in 2008 and the second mission, Chandrayaan-2, in 2019. The third mission, Chandrayaan-3, was launched in July 2023 and its Vikram lander carrying the Pragyan rover successfully soft-landed on the Moon on 23 August 2023. With this mission, India became the first country in the world to achieve a landing near the little explored Moon's south pole. To mark this success, the Government of India declared 23 August to be celebrated as 'National Space Day' in India. A fourth mission, Chandrayaan-4, is being planned which aims to bring back soil and rock samples from the Moon.
texts.text: To improve our understanding of the Moon, India's first mission to the Moon, Chandrayaan-1, was launched in 2008 and the second mission, Chandrayaan-2, in 2019. The third mission, Chandrayaan-3, was launched in July 2023 and its Vikram lander carrying the Pragyan rover successfully soft-landed on the Moon on 23 August 2023. With this mission, India became the first country in the world to achieve a landing near the little explored Moon's south pole. To mark this success, the Government of India declared 23 August to be celebrated as 'National Space Day' in India. A fourth mission, Chandrayaan-4, is being planned which aims to bring back soil and rock samples from the Moon.
